narrative: As a white male applicant with a full-time study plan ahead, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.30, LSAT score of 23.50, low family income bracket, and school decile rankings from early years—and estimated a 64% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this prediction to prioritize admits likely to pass and succeed in practice. The three biggest factors driving this decision were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your undergraduate GPA, in that order of influence. Your Year 3 decile of 1—far below the typical student's around the 5th or 6th decile—stood out as the strongest push toward predicting failure, signaling to the model that your later academic performance lagged significantly behind peers. Your LSAT score of 23.50, much lower than the average of about 37, reinforced this concern by suggesting challenges with the standardized reasoning skills key to bar success. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.30, while below the average 3.21, actually nudged the prediction slightly toward passing compared to what the model expected for someone with your other traits. Overall, these elements made your profile riskier than most applicants, who tend to have stronger mid-to-late academic showings and higher entrance exam scores. Your low family income and full-time status, common among 93% of students, played lesser roles here. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted coursework or tutoring, and exploring programs with support for students from similar income backgrounds. Building a standout personal statement highlighting your resilience could also help reframe your story for future applications. With focused improvements, you can shift the odds in your favor next time.
